# Why Reproducible Research {#sec-why}

::: {.callout-note}
## Sources

PHB 243B Lecture 2 (`~/Dropbox/prj/tch/02-phb243b-biostat/lec02/`,
including `lecture_2.qmd`, `team-science-skills.qmd`,
`renv.pdf`, `rrtools.pdf`).
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-01.

1. What does 'reproducible' mean in the context of a
   statistical analysis, and how is it different from
   'replicable'?
2. What three artefacts must you share to make an analysis
   fully reproducible?
3. Why is a reproducible analysis not necessarily a correct
   analysis? Give an example where the two diverge.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Distinguish among reproducibility, replicability, and
  correctness.
- List the ingredients of a minimum reproducible compendium.
- Argue the case for reproducibility to a sceptical principal
  investigator.
- Diagnose the common threats to reproducibility: missing
  seeds, unpinned package versions, undocumented manual steps.

## Orientation

Reproducibility is the lowest bar a scientific analysis must
clear. It says nothing about whether the analysis is correct,
important, or even sensible; it only says that a second
analyst with the same inputs can obtain the same outputs. And
yet a substantial minority of published biomedical analyses
fail this bar when audited. Fixing that is cheap but only if
you build in the habits up front.

## What 'reproducible' actually means

TODO: reproducibility vs replicability (Goodman, Fanelli, and
Ioannidis 2016); the Stodden/Claerbout hierarchy; why the
biostatistician is the team member responsible.

## The anatomy of a reproducible analysis

TODO: code + data + environment; minimum compendium; when
each is missing.

## Common failure modes

TODO: unpinned seeds, manual steps, 'works on my machine',
floating dependencies.

## The cost-benefit case

TODO: reproducibility as selfishness (future-you), not
altruism.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste the methods section of a recent biomedical paper and
   ask the LLM to list every piece of information you would
   need to reproduce the primary analysis. Score the paper:
   fraction of items present vs absent.
2. Ask the LLM to design a minimum reproducible compendium for
   a small clinical trial analysis. Does it mention
   `renv.lock`, a Dockerfile, a README, and a session-info
   file?
3. Give the LLM a plausible but incomplete R script and ask
   'what would prevent this from running on another machine?'
   Compare its answer to your own audit.
:::

## Exercises

1. Take the last analysis you wrote. List every piece of
   information that is not in the script but would be needed
   to reproduce the output. For each item, say how you would
   capture it programmatically.
2. Find a published paper in your field with public code. Try
   to reproduce one figure. Log what went wrong.
3. Write a one-page policy statement for a research group on
   what 'reproducible' means and what every team member must
   do before submitting an analysis.

## Quiz answers {#sec-quiz-answers-01}

1. A reproducible analysis is one where a collaborator given
   the same data and code can obtain identical output on their
   own machine. Replicable is stronger: it means the
   conclusions hold when the study is repeated with new data.
   Reproducibility is about the computational chain;
   replicability is about the scientific claim.
2. Code, data, and the computational environment (R version,
   package versions, operating system). Without all three,
   results may differ silently on another machine.
3. Reproducible means the analysis can be run again and
   produce the same answer. A correct analysis uses a valid
   statistical method on appropriate data. A reproducible
   analysis can still be wrong (e.g., it consistently applies
   the wrong test); a correct analysis can fail reproducibility
   if random seeds are not set or package versions are not
   pinned.
